title: Dealing With a Coworker You Can't Confront
dek: When the morning meeting becomes a somatic trap, your body remembers what your mouth couldn't say. Here is how to metabolize the fury of the unsaid before it settles in your jaw and psoas.
bodyMarkdown:
## Section 1


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.233Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

The morning meeting begins just like every other: the bright fluorescent light buzzes overhead, slicing through the cool, recycled air of the conference room. You sit rigid in your chair, the edge digging into your sacrum, your eyes on the speaker but your body elsewhere. It’s the moment when she - the coworker you can’t bring yourself to confront - utters that barely audible, sharp-as-a-razor comment. A microaggression dressed in the guise of a joke, or maybe just a careless aside, but it lands with the weight of a fist pressed hard across your masseter muscle. Your jaw clenches reflexively, the muscles taut and unyielding, as if bracing against some invisible force. The memory of that slight digs into the connective tissue beneath your skin, each fiber tightening like the strings of a pulled bow.

The subtle sting doesn’t stop there. Beneath your ribs, your diaphragm flinches, disrupted by a hollow drop in your stomach that feels like a coil unwinding, but in reverse - contracting and knotting itself tighter. You taste the faint metallic tang of adrenaline at the back of your throat, the subtle shift in your saliva’s texture. The room’s stale scent mingles with the faint trace of your own sweat, a reminder that your body is already responding, even before your mind can catch up. This is not just irritation; this is your nervous system lighting up, a primal warning encoded deep in your fascia, telling you something is wrong, something unspeakable.

You swallow hard, an effort to recalibrate, to smooth over the invisible crack widening between you and the coworker who seems to thrive in this shadowed space of unspoken tension. But the body remembers, as Thich Nhat Hanh taught - anger is energy in motion, a message from the body’s wisdom that can’t be silenced without consequence. Your trapezius muscles tighten, the levator scapulae pulling your shoulders toward your ears, and you realize: this unsaid conflict is living in you, in every subtle twitch and tightening, even as you sit frozen in the polite stillness of the meeting. The question isn’t whether you feel it - it’s what you will do with that storm locked inside your own flesh.

## Section 2

You sit there, jaw clenched, the masseter knotting like a taut rope just beneath your cheekbone, as your coworker drones on, oblivious to the tightening coil in your belly. Your psoas - the deep-seated muscle running from the lumbar vertebrae to the femur, that primal engine of fight or flight - fires in a desperate, silent protest. It wants you to move, to run, to escape the scene. Instead, you remain trapped in the office chair, the plastic cold against your skin, your hips pinched and immobile. This is the biology of the trap, and it’s not your failure or your weakness. It’s what Bessel van der Kolk calls the body’s “hidden trauma,” the way your nervous system encodes threat when your options to express or flee are denied.

The vagus nerve, that sprawling cranial nerve winding from your brainstem down through your throat, heart, lungs, and gut, is the unsung messenger here. It carries the echoes of your body’s ancient survival strategies, flicking between states of alarm and shutdown. When confrontation is impossible, your vagus nerve can pull your system into freeze - a numbing, immobilized state where your diaphragm tightens, your breath shallows, and your levator scapulae - those small muscles that cradle your neck - hunker down in a protective spasm. You might notice a faint tightness creeping along your trapezius, as if your shoulders are shrugging against invisible threats. This is the biology of feeling stuck: your body knows you’re in danger, even if your mind insists you’re just “being professional.”

Van der Kolk’s work teaches us that trauma and stress lodge themselves not in abstract memory but in the fascia and muscles - the psoas remembers the need to run just as vividly as the mind remembers the slight. Your nervous system doesn’t parse “office politics” as a distinct category separate from “life or death.” It responds to the constriction of the psoas and the hesitation of the breath with a full-body alarm, even as you force yourself to smile and nod. This is not pathology. This is biology. Your body is doing its job, shouting through tight muscles and shallow breaths: “This situation is unsafe.” And until you listen - really listen - to what your body is saying, that coil will continue to wind tighter, waiting for release.

## Section 3

The body is keyed up, the trapezius knots tightening like a noose at the base of your skull, the levator scapulae screaming in silent protest as you sit across from that coworker you can’t confront. Your system is caught in a restless stasis - fight is off the table, flight unavailable, and freeze is the default but unbearable. In these moments, your nervous system is screaming for a safe harbor, a way to temper the biochemical storm brewing beneath your skin. This is where herbs - true nervines and adaptogens - become your allies, not as a magic wand but as a steadying hand on the shoulder of your overtaxed parasympathetic nervous system.

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) is a stalwart in this realm. This root, revered in Ayurvedic tradition, doesn’t sedate or dull; it bolsters resilience. When your diaphragm tightens with shallow breath and your psoas clenches with anxiety, Ashwagandha whispers to your hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, coaxing it toward balance. It’s not about numbing the anger or the frustration swelling like a storm behind your eyes; it’s about helping your body say, “I can handle this,” when every muscle insists otherwise. [Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20), for example, is a clean, potent tincture that respects the integrity of the plant’s adaptogenic qualities. You can find it here - a reliable ally when the clock ticks through those long, clenched hours.

Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora), a nervine of the highest order, is your evening companion when the masseter muscles refuse to unclench and the mind races past midnight. It’s a plant ally that soothes the nervous system without dulling your edge, easing the tension in your jaw and the relentless tightness threading through the fascia around your temples. Unlike pharmaceuticals that silence the signal, Skullcap tunes it down just enough for you to hear your body’s deeper messages beneath the static. When you take Skullcap in a gentle infusion or tincture, you’re inviting your nervous system to relax its grip, allowing the anger stored in your fascia to soften, not disappear.

These herbs do not erase your experience of that coworker or the anger they ignite. Instead, they support the biology of your response, buffering the cascade of cortisol, smoothing the contracted muscles, and reminding your nervous system that you have options beyond fight, flight, or freeze - even when your environment offers none. This is not about submission; it’s about survival with sovereignty. The body remembers, and with the right herbal allies, you can nurture that memory into strength rather than silence.

## Section 4

You feel the tight coil deep in your forearm when you think about that coworker - the one whose presence scrapes the lining of your stomach raw, whose words echo like a slow drip of acid at midnight. This isn’t random irritation; this is your anger - raw, pulsing, and alive - sending urgent signals through the fascia and muscle, demanding you listen. Karla McLaren, whose work on anger I return to again and again, reminds us that anger is not the enemy. It is the body’s ancient, wise boundary-setter, a sentinel standing guard over your inner landscape. It’s not here to punish or shame you for feeling, but to protect you from violation.

When you can’t confront this coworker directly - whether because the power dynamics are skewed, or the workplace culture is toxic, or simply because your body tightens into a fist at the thought - you might feel stuck, trapped under a ceiling of unspoken fury. But that anger is still working, still messaging. It anchors itself in the masseter muscle, clenched jaw reverberating beneath your cheekbones. It thickens in the diaphragm, pressing down like a lead weight just beneath your ribs, making breath shallow and urgent. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your body knows you need distance, clarity, protection.

Listen to what your anger is trying to teach you. It’s saying: “This is too much. This is not safe.” It’s pointing to the levator scapulae, stiff and twitching at the base of your neck, where your body is holding the tension of needing to defend space you can’t currently claim. It’s asking you to acknowledge where your boundaries have been breached, even if you can’t articulate it out loud yet. In the quiet, at 3 a.m. when the world is still and the night air smells faintly of damp earth and something green and growing, you can feel the truth of this boundary, pressed against your skin like the cool edge of a nettle leaf.

You don’t have to confront this anger with words or confrontation right now. You can hold it in your body, honor its messages, and begin to tend to those tender, inflamed places with awareness and care. The anger is not a curse; it is your body’s way of saying, “Here lies the line.” Trust that. Trust the wisdom in that ache and tightness and fire. It’s the first step toward reclaiming your space, even if you must do so silently for now.

## Section 5

You’re standing in the bathroom stall - maybe it’s noon or three-thirty, the momentum of the workday thick around you like humid air. The light hum of fluorescent bulbs presses down, the faint antiseptic sting of soap and hand sanitizer clings to your skin. This is your moment, even if it’s just for two minutes, to let the tightness that’s been coiling behind your ribs loosen its grip. You can’t speak. You can’t confront. But you can move the energy trapped in your body, the clenched masseter around your jaw, the shallow diaphragm pulling tight like a clenched fist beneath your ribs.

First, sink down in the stall, feet flat on the cold tile floor. Feel the hard surface beneath your arches and heels. This connection grounds you. The ground doesn’t shift away when your thoughts spiral; it holds you. Let your toes spread wide, your heels press down, and notice the subtle shifts in the soleus and gastrocnemius muscles in your calves as your weight roots you. Your feet remember safety even when your mind protests otherwise.

Now, bring your attention to your jaw. The masseter muscle, thick and strong, clenched tight in that silent rage or irritation you dare not voice. Place your fingertips gently on the sides of your face, just below your cheekbones. Breathe into that space. As you inhale, imagine the muscle fibers softening, the tension melting like wax under a low flame. On your exhale, let your jaw part just slightly, teeth no longer locked but hovering, lips softly parted, a barely-there release. The body remembers this - jaw locked in silent fury is an old habit, but so is letting go, so is surrendering to the natural ebb of breath and muscle.

Drop your shoulders, the trapezius and levator scapulae often stiffen when you’re holding in anger you can’t speak. Let gravity pull them down from your ears, even if just a millimeter. Feel your diaphragm, that dome of muscle beneath your ribs, expand as you inhale deeply through your nose, filling the lungs with the coolness of the bathroom air. The breath doesn’t just fill you; it moves the energy stuck in your solar plexus, the place where anger often festers, unspoken.

This brief somatic reset - feet grounded, jaw releasing, shoulders dropping, breath expanding - is your rebellion against the oppression of silence. It’s not about suppressing or ignoring your fury; it’s about honoring its presence in a way that is visible only to your body. Later, when you return to your desk, that knot of unspoken words will still be there, but it will have shifted, loosened, less likely to calcify into bitterness or shame. This is the body’s wisdom, the quiet work of anger tending when confrontation isn’t possible.

## Section 6

The Cost of Swallowing It: The Physical Toll of Unexpressed Anger

You sit at your desk, jaw clenched so tightly the masseter muscle hums with fatigue. The corners of your mouth pull down against an invisible resistance, a silent war waged beneath your skin. This is the cost of swallowing your anger - the body’s quiet rebellion when words stay trapped in the throat. Gabor Maté’s teaching, “The Body Says No,” echoes here like a warning bell. When you deny your own felt experience, your body does not forget; it archives it, files it away in muscle tension, in the slow burn of inflammation.

By late afternoon, a dull headache presses behind your eyes, a band of tight trapezius and levator scapulae muscles pulling your skull downward. It’s no coincidence that these muscles are the very ones that contract when you hold in rage. Every suppressed “no” you don’t speak sets up camp in these small muscles, turning them into sentinels of your unvoiced fury. Your diaphragm, that crucial muscle of breath and life, becomes shallow and uneven. With each tight, clipped inhale, you send a signal to your nervous system that something is wrong, even if your lips are smiling and your voice is steady.

Digestive distress often follows, as if your gut is trying to process the unprocessed. The stomach’s lining, delicate and sensitive, reacts to the cortisol released by chronic stress. You notice the familiar gnawing, the queasy twist in your lower abdomen, or perhaps the sluggishness that drags you down after lunch. This is no coincidence. The enteric nervous system - the “second brain” in your gut - absorbs your emotional load, translating your anger into cramps, bloating, or that stubborn constipation that feels like your body is holding on to what it cannot release.

This is not pathology; this is biology. Your body is not punishing you for your anger; it is trying to tell you something vital. It remembers the moments you bit back your words, the times you swallowed the scorch of fury to keep the peace. It carries these moments in the fascia wrapped tightly around your ribs, in the psoas muscle that tenses beneath your belly, in the subtle grinding of your teeth long after the workday ends. To listen is to begin the unspooling, to honor the message beneath the ache. The body is not your enemy; it is the messenger you have yet to hear.

## Section 7

You hold your breath when she corners you by the copier, the tight coil in your trapezius refusing to loosen. The masseter clenches like a fist against your molars, a silent scream you dare not voice. This is the body speaking its own brutal truth: anger held is anger stored, fermenting quietly in the liver’s dark chambers. Traditional Chinese Medicine knows this well - the liver is not just an organ but the seat of anger, the wellspring of frustration that moves the qi, the life force, through your entire being. When that flow is stuck or stagnant, the body wages a quiet war, tightening the fascia around your ribs, knotting the levator scapulae, whispering alerts in the back of your throat.

In these moments, when confrontation is impossible and the air in the office feels too thick to breathe, you need an ally that speaks the language of the liver. Bitter herbs are the answer, the sharp, grounding medicine that cuts through the stagnation. They don’t coddle; they stimulate, coaxing the liver to release its grip, to let the trapped energy flow again. Think of dandelion root, burdock, or even the gentle, steadying presence of chamomile. [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a simple, profound tool here. It’s not just a sleepy-time tea; it’s a nervine that gently nudges the liver, easing the tension that wraps around your diaphragm like a vice.

When you sip that bitter brew, notice the immediate somatic shift. The warmth travels down your esophagus, a slow, deliberate grounding. The bitter taste on your tongue signals the vagus nerve, sending a message down to the digestive tract: “Wake up, move, release.” The liver responds, the tight knot in your right side softening just a fraction. The breath deepens, the diaphragm dropping lower, no longer held hostage by the unsaid words. This is not about fixing the coworker or the situation; it’s about tending to the internal landscape, giving your body the support it needs to metabolize the anger rather than swallow it whole.

## Section 8

You sit at your desk, the fluorescent lights humming a low, irritating frequency above you. The coworker you can’t confront has just walked past, leaving a trail of unspoken tension in their wake. Your body responds before your mind even registers the slight. The masseter muscle in your jaw clenches, tight and unyielding, a silent protest against the words you swallow. Your psoas, deep in your core, tightens as if preparing to flee, but you remain anchored to your ergonomic chair. This is the somatic reality of unexpressed anger - a physical holding pattern that demands your attention, not your judgment.

Resmaa Menakem’s work on settling the body is crucial here. He teaches us that the body holds the score, that trauma and stress are not just mental constructs but physical realities lodged in our tissues. When you reframe the confrontation, you shift the focus from the external trigger - the coworker - to your internal landscape. It’s not about them; it’s about your own internal boundary. Your body is signaling that a line has been crossed, and the tightness in your chest, the shallow breathing, the rigid posture - these are all messages from your nervous system, demanding to be heard.

To settle the body, you must first acknowledge the physical sensation of the anger. Notice the heat rising in your neck, the subtle tremor in your hands. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your body is doing exactly what it was designed to do - protect you. But when confrontation is impossible, you must find a way to metabolize that energy. Menakem suggests grounding practices, like feeling the weight of your feet on the floor, or the solid support of the chair beneath you. This simple act of anchoring can begin to down-regulate your nervous system, signaling to your body that you are safe, even in the presence of the trigger.

Reframing the confrontation means recognizing that your anger is a valid, necessary response to a boundary violation. It’s a call to action, not a call to arms. By tending to your body’s needs - by settling the nervous system, by acknowledging the physical toll of the unsaid - you reclaim your power. You may not be able to confront the coworker, but you can confront the tension in your own body. You can choose to listen to the wisdom of your muscles, your fascia, your breath, and in doing so, you honor the boundary that was crossed, even if you never speak a word.

## Section 9

The evening routine is not a luxury; it is a necessity, a deliberate act of washing off the day’s unexpressed anger before it calcifies in your joints. You step into the bathroom, the air already thick with the promise of release. The water must be hot - not just warm, but hot enough to shock the system, to demand the attention of every nerve ending on your skin. As the water hits your shoulders, you feel the immediate, involuntary softening of the trapezius, the muscle that has been holding the weight of every unspoken word, every swallowed retort, since 9 a.m.

You reach for the soap, not just any soap, but one that smells of earth and root, something grounding. The scent of cedar or vetiver fills the small space, anchoring you in the present moment, pulling you away from the sterile, fluorescent-lit memory of the office. You scrub, not gently, but with intention, working the lather into the tight knots of your levator scapulae, the muscles that have been pulling your shoulders up toward your ears all day. This is not just cleansing; this is an exorcism of the day’s accumulated tension, a physical unspooling of the anger that has been winding itself tight in your fascia.

After the shower, while your skin is still damp and receptive, you reach for the oil. [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a potent ally here. Arnica, a plant known for its ability to heal bruises and soothe deep muscle aches, is exactly what your body needs. You massage it into your skin, focusing on the areas that hold the most tension - the jaw, the neck, the lower back. The oil is warm, the scent herbaceous and medicinal, a reminder that healing is a physical process, not just a mental one. You work the oil into the masseter muscle, feeling the tight, fibrous bands begin to yield under your fingers.

This routine is your sanctuary, a time to honor the body’s wisdom and its need for release. You are not just washing off the day; you are metabolizing the anger, transforming it from a toxic, stagnant energy into something fluid and manageable. As you step out of the bathroom, the air cooler now, you feel a subtle shift. The anger is still there, perhaps, but it is no longer trapped in your muscles. It has been acknowledged, tended to, and allowed to move through you, leaving you lighter, more grounded, and ready to face whatever tomorrow may bring.

## Section 10

**Q: How do I know if my anger is actually stuck in my body, or if I’m just stressed?**

**A:** The body doesn’t lie, and it rarely whispers when it’s holding onto unexpressed anger. Stress often feels like a generalized buzzing - a frantic, scattered energy that makes your mind race and your breath shallow. But stuck anger is specific. It’s the tight, unyielding knot in your masseter muscle that makes your jaw ache by 3 p.m. It’s the rigid, defensive posture of your levator scapulae, pulling your shoulders up as if bracing for a blow. It’s the deep, primal clench of your psoas, preparing you to fight or flee when neither is an option. If you feel a localized, intense tension that doesn’t dissipate with a deep breath or a stretch, that’s your body holding onto the fury you couldn’t voice.

**Q: I can’t confront my coworker because they’re my superior. How do I process this anger without risking my job?**

**A:** This is the reality for many, and it’s crucial to understand that processing anger doesn’t always mean expressing it to the person who triggered it. Your body needs to metabolize the energy, not necessarily deliver a message. When confrontation is unsafe or impossible, focus on somatic release. Use the bathroom stall reset - ground your feet, release your jaw, and breathe deeply into your diaphragm. Outside of work, engage in practices that allow your body to complete the stress cycle: vigorous exercise, deep tissue massage, or even screaming into a pillow. The goal is to move the energy out of your fascia and muscles, so it doesn’t turn into chronic pain or illness.

**Q: Can herbs really help with anger, or is it just a placebo effect?**

**A:** Herbs are not a placebo; they are profound, biochemical allies that interact directly with your nervous system. When you’re holding onto anger, your sympathetic nervous system is in overdrive, flooding your body with cortisol and adrenaline. Nervines like Skullcap and adaptogens like Ashwagandha don’t magically erase the anger, but they do help regulate your body’s response to it. They soothe the frayed edges of your nervous system, allowing your parasympathetic nervous system (the "rest and digest" state) to come back online. This shift in your biology gives you the space to process the anger somatically, rather than just reacting to it or suppressing it further.

**Q: What if I’ve been swallowing my anger for years? Is it too late to release it?**

**A:** It is never too late. The body is an incredible archive, but it is also a dynamic, self-healing system. Years of suppressed anger may have calcified into chronic tension, digestive issues, or even autoimmune responses, but the fascia and muscles can always learn to let go. Start small. You don’t need to excavate decades of fury all at once. Begin by noticing where you hold tension today. Use the evening routine with Arnica oil to gently coax those tight muscles into softening. Work with a somatic therapist or an herbalist who understands the physical toll of unexpressed emotion. Your body has been holding this for you; it will also guide you in how to release it, layer by layer.
